prior_learning: Students should be competent at using a formal written method for all 4 operations. Check this before moving onto problem solving using the operations.
With index laws it is important to teach it with understanding rather than just a rule – link it into prior knowledge of cancellation of terms.
Be explicit that equations and formulas are really the same things with different names.
Students should know and be able to use inequality symbols – check this before problem solving with inequalities.
Check students are able to order directed numbers before calculating with them.
common_misconceptions: Clarify that BODMAS/BIDMAS is a mathematical convention so that we don’t get different answers
Check calculator does BODMAS/BIDMAS or students will need to use brackets on calculators
With index laws it is important to teach it with understanding rather than just a rule – link it into prior knowledge of cancellation of terms.
Students often confuse squaring with multiplying by 2 – they believe 3 x 2 = 32
Be specific with language around indices. Some students know them as powers.

learning_outcomes: Solve problems involving written methods of addition and subtraction with whole numbers
Solve problems involving written methods of addition and subtraction with decimals
key_knowledge_components: I can solve number problems involving addition, subtraction, multiplications and division
I can compare and order whole numbers and decimals.

key_vocab: Add, subtract, total, difference, minus, altogether,

key_questions: Is the above an addition or subtraction question? What word indicates which it is? (Difference)
How is adding/subtracting decimals different to whole numbers?
What do you need to remember when adding/subtracting decimals? (The decimal points all need to be directly underneath each other eg
Give students incorrect calculations and ask them to find the errors.
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start by focusing on common errors that students make with whole number written addition and subtraction. Spend time diagnosing misconceptions before giving students some to practise. Determine the questions depending on different students’ skill. Give students worded questions where they need to determine if it’s subtraction or addition and what word in the question indicates that eg In the example above it’s ‘how many more’ that indicates it’s…

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Use lots of questioning to diagnose and address misconceptions and then move onto more complex questions – don’t move on though until students understand. Give students questions with the calculation set up horizontally eg., 3.24 + 45.6 so t…

key_questions: What patterns do you notice? Try and generalise.
Which would be the correct answer and why?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Talk to students about using what the know to help them work out what they don’t know eg 7 x 5 = 35 so 7 x 0.5 = 3.5.  0.5 is the same as a half so the question is the same as half of 7. Alternatively, encourage students to look for patterns eg 7 x 0.5 = 0.7 x 0.5 = 7 x 0.05 =

learning_outcomes: Solve problems involving written methods of division with whole numbers
Solve problems involving written methods of division with decimals
key_knowledge_components: I can solve number problems involving addition, subtraction, multiplications and division

key_questions: How do you set this question up? What do we call this method? (Bus stop)
What is the next step? Do you think X has done this question correctly? Why? How do you know?
What mistake has X made? How would you fix this error?
What do we do with the remainder? What do we do with the remainder at the end of the question?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start with a 3-digit number divided by a whole number divisor (967 and get a student to demonstrate using the bus stop method. Use questioning throughout to clear up any misconceptions regarding the method. See what the student who is demonstrating does with the remainder. Ask students how to deal with the remainder – some will only be able to leave it as a remainder whereas others may be ready to move onto writing it as a decimal. For this less…

Students need to be competent at a written method of division of whole numbers before trying with decimals. Until they are confident they should leave any remainder as R4 for remainder 4 etc Some students will find it easier if they write ou…

key_questions: 12 ÷ 3 = 4, so what would 12 ÷0.3 =?
How would you scale this question up to make it easier?
(multiply everything by 100 so that you are not dividing by a decimal eg  80 ÷ 5 = 0.8 ÷ 0.05)

assessment.afl_notes_summary: Suggested teaching  activities, pedagogy and assessment to inform next steps: Re-visit previous lesson on using bus stop method at the difficulty that students were working at. The purpose of this lesson is to provide a strategy for when the divisor is also a decimal. Build on from previous lesson that you can use your answer to one question to help with another eg 12 ÷ 3 = 4, so what would 12 ÷0.3 =? Show students that when you have a decimal a…

learning_outcomes: Compare 2 numbers using =, ≠, <, >, ≤, ≥;
Order numbers including whole numbers, negatives, mixed fractions, decimals
key_knowledge_components: I can compare and order whole numbers and decimals.

key_vocab: Place value, units, tens, hundreds, thousands, tenths, hundredths, thousandths, partition, recombine, lass than, greater than, inequality, mental method, written method, jotting, infinity ∞, ascending, descending

key_questions: Which is bigger? Smaller? How do you know?
What does this symbol mean <?
What does this symbol mean ≤ ?
Which is bigger 0.70 or 0.9?
Which is smaller 0.50 or 0.5? Why?  How do you know these are the same?
Which is bigger 0.56 0r 43%?
Put the following numbers in order:
Which is bigger ¾ or 0.72?
Which is bigger ¾ or 72%?
Place the following numbers on the number line 54%, 0.25, 2/5, 0.66, 3/4:
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start with students placing a decimal number between 0 and 1 on a scale to order them. Give each student a different decimal number. Give some questions where students put numbers in ascending/descending order – us a PV table if necessary As soon as students are secure with ordering decimals introduce negatives and simple fractions. Give problem solving questions where they first need to convert units eg

key_questions: How would I convert a fraction to a decimal?
How would I convert a percentage to a decimal?
Which is bigger 0.74 or ? How do you know? What is as a decimal?
What happens when you convert   or  to a decimal using the bus stop method?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: This lesson builds on the previous one but students will need to convert fractions and percentages to a decimal before ordering. Start with basic conversions that students need to know by rote eg ½ = 0.5 = 50% = 0.25 = 25% = 0.75 = 75% = 0.10 = 10%

key_knowledge_components: I know mathematical rules for all 4 operations with directed number
I can calculate addition, subtraction, multiplications and division that involve negative numbers.

key_questions: Progression in questions:
What is
3 -5 ? (subtracting units)
-6 + 4? (adding units)
30 – 50 ?(subtracting in tens)
-60 + 40? (adding in tens)
30 – 57?  (subtracting tens and then units)
-60 + 43? (adding in tens and units)
36 – 57? (subtracting in tens but not multiples of 10 and then units)
-68 + 43? (adding in 10s but not multiples of 10 and then units)
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Use number lines to help students visualise. They should be familiar with both horizontal and vertical number lines.  Start with questions such as 3 – 8 and -5 + 6 and use a number line divided into units. Then move onto number lines divided into tens and give questions such as 30 -80, -50 + 60 before moving to 30 – 83 and -50 + 64 before finally using examples like 36-83 and -52 + 64. The progression between these is important. (See key questio…

key_knowledge_components: I know mathematical rules for all 4 operations with directed number
I can calculate addition, subtraction, multiplications and division that involve negative numbers.

key_questions: What happens to the answer when you multiply 2 negative numbers together?
What about when you divide 2 negative numbers?
What about if just one number is negative? What happens to the answer?
Can you generalise? What would the rules be?
In -5 x -2 x -3 would the answer be negative or positive? Why?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Check what students already know before teaching this lesson so that you know where to pitch it. Using the table above students can start to look for patterns in order to generalise and identify the rules . Give students some to practise before moving onto questions like -5 x -2 x -3. Give questions that are applicable to students’ understanding eg: Incorporate some recall of other topics eg multiplying fractions as above.

learning_outcomes: Knowing the order of operations
Solving problems using BODMAS/BIDMAS
Solving more complex problems using BODMAS/BIDMAS
Adding brackets to a calculation so that answers are correct/identifying errors in calculations involving BODMADS/BIDMAS
key_knowledge_components: I can use BIDMAS to work out calculations including brackets,  powers, roots and fractions.

key_questions: What is the answer to 7 + 3 x 2?
Can you find another?
How do we know which is the correct answer?
Where should the brackets go to make this correct?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start with a question like the one above and ask for answers? You could get 20 or 13. The reason we use BIDMAS is so that we have an agreed order to do the operations in so that we don’t get more than one answer. Explain that BIDMAS is just a mathematical convention (an agreed practice) that we use so that we don’t end out with different answers. Students should know from previous years what each letter in BIDMAS stands for but re-cap on this to…

learning_outcomes: Understanding square and cube numbers
Finding square and cubic roots
Use of calculator with squares, cubes and roots
Finding the law of indices
ya x yb = yab
Finding the law of indices
ya ÷ yb = ya-b
Using laws of indices to simplify
key_knowledge_components: I know the laws of indices including with negative indices
I can use power laws and understand the use of negative powers.
I know the laws of indices including with negative indices
I can use power laws and understand the use of negative powers.

key_vocab: Square, cube, square root, cube root,
Index notation, indices, power, evaluate, calculate, base

key_questions: What is a square number? How can they be calculated?
What is a cube number?
What is the opposite of squaring? (square root)
The answer was 64, what was the question?
What is the answer to -5 squared?
How do I do this calculation using a calculator?
Why do I get the answer -25 when I calculate -52? The answer should be 25. How do I avoid this happening? (Use the brackets on the calculator).
What is a ‘base’ when I am talking about indices?
How can I write 2 x 2 x 3 x 3 x 3 x 3 x 2 using indices?
Simplify
(The bases are different so it’s not possible to simplify the above using indes laws).
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Clarify what square numbers are and check students can both square numbers and recognise a square number. Then introduce cube numbers. Clarify notation eg., 32 means 3 x 3 and 43 = 4 x 4 x 4. Get students to identify square roots and cubic roots of numbers.  Check students know how to use the square, cube, square root and cubic root on a calculator Then introduce other powers and roots and show where the functions are on the calculator: Give stu…

It is a common misconception that squaring is the same as multiplying by 2. Check students understand that squaring is multiplying by itself. key_questions: How would you read this number?
When would we write numbers in standard form?

teaching.suggested_activities_summary: You may need to re-cap multiplying and dividing by powers of 10 at the beginning of the lesson. Check with a recall prior to this lesson so that you pitch this lesson appropriately. Introduce standard form as a way of writing very big numbers so that it doesn’t take so long to write them out in full. Model how to write numbers in standard form eg., These numbers are written in standard form: Check students understand how to write an ordinary num…

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Avoid saying ‘you just add a zero’ when you multiply by 10 as this causes a misconception when you are working with decimals.  Alternatively, each digit gets 1 place bigger when you multiply by 10. teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Introduce negative powers of 10 and get students to look at the pattern to generalise eg 10 -1 = 0.1, 10-2 = 0.01, 10-3  = 0.001 etc Explain to students that you can also use standard form to write very small numbers: Give them some examples to both write in standard form but also in ordinary form.
